title: Abuse: Aiming for Biblical Balance (Part 2)
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: Introduction: Review and Three Foundational Agreements
subpoints: Opening Scripture: Psalm 56 — a song of the oppressed, appropriate for those struggling with abuse

Review of Part 1's three foundational agreements: marriage is worth protecting, some believers are called to stay in difficult marriages (1 Peter 3:1-2; 1 Cor. 7), victims of oppression must be protected
Caution about women who choose to stay in difficult marriages for Christ's sake — comparable to missionaries in dangerous fields; we should not look down on such faithfulness
Review of overstatements 1-4: always believe the victim; if you feel abused you were abused; victims bear no fault at any point; if you feel controlled leave

outline.title: Overstatement 5: Abusers Can Never Change
subpoints: Valid concern: some women have been wrongly told that their behavior causes their husband's violence — some men are stubbornly sinful regardless (Prov. 16:29; Prov. 29:1)
Danger: categorical hopelessness contradicts the gospel — 1 Corinthians 6:9-11: 'and such were some of you'; Paul himself was 'the chief abuser of Christians' before his conversion
True repentance vs. worldly sorrow: 2 Corinthians 7:9-11; Jim Newheiser's seven traits of godly repentance vs. seven traits of worldly sorrow
Godly repentance: God-focused, hates sin, submits to accountability, changed heart producing fruit, patiently accepts consequences, accepts full responsibility, concerned for others

outline.title: Overstatement 6: Scriptures Have Been Weaponized — Don't Use Them
subpoints: Valid concern: the Bible has been misused by oppressive husbands and careless counselors — submission texts used to send abused women back into danger
Danger: removing Scripture removes Christ; what greater wisdom would you substitute? Psalm 19:7-10 — the law of the Lord is more to be desired than gold, sweeter than honey

The Scriptures call themselves a weapon (Eph. 6:17) — the answer to misuse is not disarmament but 'rightly weaponizing' them; Jesus answered the devil's misuse of Scripture with Scripture rightly applied
Better approach: counselors should listen carefully first (Prov. 18:13), draw out the person (Prov. 20:5), and then apply Scripture with compassion and accuracy

outline.title: Overstatement 7: Power and Authority Must Be Resisted
subpoints: The claim: any relationship with a difference in authority is by definition abusive — including biblical headship in marriage
Origin: Marxist critical theory (oppressor/oppressed dialectic applied to gender); this is not a Christian framework
Danger: evangelical embrace of egalitarianism on these grounds dismantles what Scripture teaches about complementary gender roles
Better approach: promote the biblical model of marriage rather than discard it because it has been misused; authority misused is corrected by accountability, not elimination

outline.title: Overstatement 8 (Deferred): Trauma-Informed Training Required
subpoints: The claim: anyone helping a victim of abuse must receive 'trauma-informed training' — a technical term that says those who have suffered trauma cannot be expected to meet normal behavioral standards
Concern: this has real application in helping those with PTSD, but the technical 'trauma-informed' model can remove all expectations from the person and exempt them from biblical standards
This overstatement will be addressed when studying the Duluth model in a future session

summary: The second Sunday school lesson in a series on abuse. After reviewing the four overstatements from Part 1 (always believe the victim; if you feel abused you were abused; victims bear no fault; if you feel controlled leave), Pastor Tito Lyro examines four more: (5) abusers can never change — refuted by 1 Corinthians 6:9-11 and the conversion of Paul; true vs. worldly repentance (Jim Newheiser's seven traits of each); (6) Scriptures have been weaponized and should not be used — the answer is rightly applying Scripture, not discarding it; (7) any authority structure is inherently abusive — identified as Marxist critical theory, not Scripture; (8) trauma-informed training required — deferred to a future session. The session ends with a definition of abuse to be developed next week.
transcript:
## Introduction: Opening Scripture and Review

We open this morning with Psalm 56 — a song of the oppressed, a song of one who is being abused. It is a great psalm for someone who is struggling with oppression to rely on, to read, to minister to their soul:

*Be merciful to me, O God, for man would swallow me up... When I am afraid, I will trust in You... In God I have put my trust; I will not be afraid. What can man do to me?*

God is for the humble and the oppressed, and he is going to vindicate all who are abused. He cares about how we think and deal with this topic, and we are to minister to both the abused and the abuser — both groups need the body of Christ.

Before we look at the next four overstatements, let me review what we covered two weeks ago.

Three foundational agreements that every Bible-believing Christian should hold: marriage is worth protecting — *what God has joined together let no man separate* (Matt. 19:6). Some believers are called to stay in difficult marriages — 1 Peter 3:1-2 instructs a believing wife on living with an unbelieving husband; 1 Corinthians 7 addresses the same. Victims of oppression must be protected — some marriages are so oppressive that those being injured have the freedom to remove themselves for safety.

A note from a conversation after last session: we gladly celebrate missionaries who go to dangerous places for Christ's sake. Yet we often look down on women who choose to stay in a difficult marriage in order to minister to their husbands. They are doing something comparable — suffering for Christ's sake in order to reach someone with the gospel. We should be careful not to despise that faithfulness.

From the four overstatements we covered: always believe the victim — better: all claims must be taken seriously and investigated; the Bible requires evidence before treating someone as guilty. If you feel abused, you were abused — better: help the person objectively evaluate what happened; not everything that feels abusive is abuse, and abuse must be defined objectively. Victims bear no fault at any point — the abuser is 100% guilty for the abuse itself, but the victim is also a sinner who may need pastoral help in her own responses (bitterness, forgiveness, etc.), not as justification for the abuse but for her own flourishing. If you feel mistreated or controlled, leave — better: distinguish degrees of sinful mistreatment and preserve marriages at all biblical cost.

## Overstatement 5: Abusers Can Never Change

This has become increasingly common in Christian and secular literature on abuse. The claim is that every abuser suffers from narcissistic personality disorder, and therefore nothing can be done — there is no hope.

The valid concern: Some women have received terrible advice that if they could just be kinder and quieter, their violent husbands would stop hurting them. There are some men who are so stubbornly sinful that no amount of kindness changes them. Proverbs 16:29 describes the violent man; Proverbs 29:1 describes the man who hardens his neck under rebuke and will be suddenly destroyed. These are real categories.

The danger: To say categorically that abusers cannot be changed is to say that you do not believe the Bible. Full stop. Look at 1 Corinthians 6:9-11: *Do you not know that the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom of God? Do not be deceived: neither the sexually immoral, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor men who practice homosexuality, nor thieves, nor the greedy, nor drunkards, nor revilers, nor swindlers will inherit the kingdom of God.* Stop there. These are life-dominating sins — the ones we think are hopeless. And then verse 11: *And such were some of you. But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus and by the Spirit of our God.*

The homosexuals, the adulterers, the revilers — changed by the gospel? Yes. When you say abusers cannot be changed, you are saying Paul is lying. What about Paul himself? He was the chief persecutor — the chief abuser — of the church. God transformed him. With God, nothing is impossible.

That said, abusers are masters of manipulation and worldly sorrow. They know how to express remorse. So we need to distinguish godly repentance from worldly sorrow. 2 Corinthians 7:9-11: *For godly grief produces a repentance that leads to salvation without regret, whereas worldly grief produces death. For see what earnestness this godly grief has produced in you, what eagerness to clear yourselves, what indignation, what fear, what longing, what zeal, what punishment!*

Jim Newheiser at Reformed Theological Seminary Charlotte has produced a helpful card comparing the seven traits of godly repentance and worldly sorrow. Godly repentance: God-focused. Hates sin, not just consequences. Submits to discipline and accountability. Changed heart that produces fruit — the behavior actually changes. Patiently accepts consequences. Fully accepts responsibility. Concerned for others.

Worldly sorrow is the opposite: Self-focused. Hates the consequences of sin. Resists accountability ('I already repented — why do you keep bringing this up?'). No changed heart — the behavior continues. Demands immediate restoration ('Tell my wife she needs to come back to me — I already repented'). Self-protective. Blames others.

As you help someone, ask: which pattern do you see? The gospel gives hope that sinners can be forgiven and transformed. Those in whose life God has worked will show signs of genuine, patient, other-focused repentance.

## Overstatement 6: Scriptures Have Been Weaponized — Don't Use Them

The valid concern is real: oppressive husbands have beaten their wives with Bible verses — primarily the submission passages in 1 Peter 3 and Ephesians 5. Pastors have used these same texts to send abused women back into dangerous situations. Some counselors have launched into biblical instruction before compassionately hearing the person's story, violating Proverbs 18:13: *If one gives an answer before he hears, it is his folly and shame.*

But the danger of this overstatement is severe: if we set aside the Scriptures, what are we going to give people? What wisdom is greater? Psalm 19:7-10: *The law of the Lord is perfect, reviving the soul; the testimony of the Lord is sure, making wise the simple... More to be desired are they than gold, even much fine gold; sweeter also than honey and drippings of the honeycomb.* Do you think a person who has been through the hell of abuse cannot benefit from the sweetness of God's Word? Do you think you are wiser than the Spirit of God?

The Scriptures call themselves a weapon — Ephesians 6:17: *the sword of the Spirit, which is the Word of God.* The Word is sharper than any two-edged sword, dividing soul and spirit. The answer to misuse is not disarmament. When the devil misused Scripture to tempt Jesus, Jesus did not respond with silence. He answered with Scripture properly applied.

Better approach: listen carefully and compassionately first. Hear the full story — Proverbs 20:5 says a counselor of understanding draws it out. Use Scripture with wisdom and care, applying passages appropriate to the situation, with compassion for the sufferer. The overall message of Scripture is Christ — who has suffered as we have, who has compassion on us in our suffering, and who offers the help we need.

## Overstatement 7: Power and Authority Must Be Resisted

This overstatement says that any relationship in which one person has authority over another is inherently abusive — including a marriage with a husband as head. By this logic, even the most Christlike husband leading the most willing wife is in an abusive relationship by definition.

It is sobering to see how many professing evangelical Christians have embraced this position, moving toward egalitarian gender roles on these grounds.

This idea did not originate in Scripture. Its origin is Marxist critical theory — the oppressor/oppressed framework applied to gender. The solution in this framework is not reformed authority but no authority: the oppressed must be given power over the oppressor. This is precisely what critical theory teaches, and it is not a Christian framework.

Better approach: promote the biblical model of marriage. The fact that authority structures have been misused does not mean authority is wrong — it means the misuse needs to be corrected. A man who misuses his authority should face accountability and discipline. Dismantling the structure harms everyone.

## Overstatement 8: Trauma-Informed Training Required (Deferred)

This is the claim that anyone who wants to help a victim of abuse must receive technical 'trauma-informed' training — a term that in its technical form says that someone who has suffered trauma cannot be expected to meet normal behavioral or relational standards.

This has legitimate application in certain situations (such as combat veterans with PTSD). But as a framework for all abuse counseling, it removes standards and expectations in ways that are not consistent with biblical teaching. We will address this more fully when we look at the Duluth model in a coming session.

Father, thank you that you are the God of the oppressed. Help us to address this topic with your wisdom, protecting the vulnerable and holding the unrepentant accountable, all for the glory of Christ. Amen.
